publications.title: Natural micropolymorphism in human leukocyte antigens provides a basis for genetic control of antigen recognition.
publications.authors: Archbold JK, Macdonald WA, Gras S, Ely LK, Miles JJ, Bell MJ, Brennan RM, Beddoe T, Wilce MC, Clements CS, Purcell AW, McCluskey J, Burrows SR, Rossjohn J.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined how single-amino-acid polymorphisms within the HLA-B44 allelic family—specifically comparing HLA-B*4405, HLA-B*4402, and HLA-B*4403—control recognition of an Epstein-Barr virus epitope (EENLLDFVRF) by TCR. The crystal structure of the TCR-HLA-B*4405 complex revealed that peptide dynamics, rather than static binding mode alone, govern preferential T cell recognition, with HLA-B*4405 enabling greater peptide flexibility than its closely related allotypes. These micropolymorphisms alter the conformational landscape of the peptide-MHC complex, providing a structural basis for how minimal sequence variation in HLA alleles can fine-tune protective immunity and explain the association between specific HLA types and disease susceptibility or viral control.
